content.text: A ReadOnly lock is an Azure Resource Manager control that prevents any write or delete operation on the locked resource. It is one of two lock types available in Azure — the other being CanNotDelete. When a ReadOnly lock is applied, users and automation can still read the resource's properties, list its contents, and interact with its data plane (SSH into a VM, query a database, read blobs) but any ARM-level mutation returns a 409 Conflict error: RequestConflict: The scope is locked.

attrs.alt: Diagram showing ReadOnly lock scope inheritance, allowed read operations, and blocked write/delete operations

content.text: ReadOnly locks block the ARM control plane — the API surface that creates, updates, and deletes resources. They do NOT block data plane operations such as reading or writing data inside the resource, connecting to a VM, or running queries against a database.

Locks can be applied at subscription, resource group, or individual resource scope. A lock applied at a resource group level is inherited by all resources within the group, meaning you do not need to lock each resource individually. The lock scope determines the blast radius: locking a subscription protects everything in it; locking a resource group protects that group's contents; locking a single resource protects only that resource.

# Apply a ReadOnly lock to a resource group
az lock create \
  --name 'prod-readonly' \
  --resource-group rg-prod \
  --lock-type ReadOnly \
  --notes 'Prevents accidental writes to production resources during incident investigation'

# Apply a ReadOnly lock to a single resource (storage account)
az lock create \
  --name 'sa-readonly' \
  --resource-group rg-prod \
  --resource-name sa-prod-data \
  --resource-type Microsoft.Storage/storageAccounts \
  --lock-type ReadOnly

# Apply a ReadOnly lock at subscription scope
az lock create \
  --name 'sub-readonly' \
  --lock-type ReadOnly \
  --scope /subscriptions/$(az account show --query id -o tsv) \
  --notes 'Monthly billing freeze — no changes during cost review period'

# List all locks in a resource group
az lock list --resource-group rg-prod -o table

# List all locks in a subscription (including inherited)
az lock list --scope /subscriptions/$(az account show --query id -o tsv) -o table

Removing a lock requires Microsoft.Authorization/locks/delete permission, which is included in Owner and User Access Administrator roles. Contributor role alone is NOT sufficient — a deliberate design so that someone who can deploy and manage resources cannot simply remove a lock and make changes. To remove a lock, identify it by name and scope, then delete it.

# Remove a lock by name at resource group scope
az lock delete \
  --name 'prod-readonly' \
  --resource-group rg-prod

# Remove a lock at subscription scope
az lock delete \
  --name 'sub-readonly' \
  --scope /subscriptions/$(az account show --query id -o tsv)

# Find the lock ID to delete via resource ID
LOCK_ID=$(az lock list \
  --resource-group rg-prod \
  --query "[?name=='prod-readonly'].id" -o tsv)
az lock delete --id $LOCK_ID

# Check who has permission to delete locks (must have Microsoft.Authorization/locks/delete)
az role assignment list \
  --scope /subscriptions/$(az account show --query id -o tsv) \
  --query '[?roleDefinitionName==`Owner` || roleDefinitionName==`User Access Administrator`].{Principal:principalName,Role:roleDefinitionName}' \
  -o table

Several Azure operations appear to be read operations but internally require a write at the ARM level, which means a ReadOnly lock blocks them unexpectedly. Understanding these edge cases is important before applying a ReadOnly lock to a live production resource group.
PATCH operation on the VM resource even though no config changes
PUT operation to update the VM's power state via ARM
Moving a resource to another RG
Write to both source and destination resource group
Adding a tag to a resource
PATCH on the resource's tag collection — tags are ARM metadata
PATCH on the storage account ARM resource
Data plane operation; bypasses ARM lock
GET on the storage data plane; bypasses ARM lock
Connection goes directly to the VM agent; not an ARM operation
SQL data plane; not blocked by ARM lock
POST to the ARM management API
If a ReadOnly lock blocks an operation you need, the correct fix is to remove the lock, perform the operation, and re-apply the lock — not to grant higher permissions to work around the lock.
Terraform: Managing ReadOnly Locks as Code

Managing locks as Terraform resources ensures they are consistently applied and version-controlled. The azurerm_management_lock resource applies to resource groups or individual resources. Because locks block Terraform from deleting the resource group during a destroy, add a lifecycle block to destroy the lock before the group.

# terraform/locks.tf

# ReadOnly lock on the production resource group
resource "azurerm_management_lock" "rg_prod_readonly" {
  name       = "prod-readonly"
  scope      = azurerm_resource_group.prod.id
  lock_level = "ReadOnly"
  notes      = "Protects production resources from accidental writes"

  # Ensure the lock is removed before the resource group is destroyed
  lifecycle {
    # Lock must be deleted before RG destroy; Terraform handles ordering automatically
    # via the dependency on azurerm_resource_group.prod.id
    prevent_destroy = false
  }
}

# ReadOnly lock on a critical storage account only
resource "azurerm_management_lock" "sa_prod_readonly" {
  name       = "sa-prod-data-readonly"
  scope      = azurerm_storage_account.prod_data.id
  lock_level = "ReadOnly"
  notes      = "Protects billing data archive from modification"
}

# To deploy without error: plan will show lock creation after resource creation
# terraform init && terraform plan && terraform apply

# To destroy: must remove lock resource first, or terraform destroy handles ordering
# terraform destroy -target=azurerm_management_lock.rg_prod_readonly
# terraform destroy

A ReadOnly lock applied manually can be removed by anyone with Owner permissions. To enforce that production resource groups always have a lock, use an Azure Policy with DeployIfNotExists effect that deploys a ReadOnly lock whenever a resource group exists without one. This is a self-healing pattern — the lock comes back automatically if someone removes it.

Azure provides two lock levels: ReadOnly (no writes or deletes) and CanNotDelete (writes allowed, deletes blocked). The choice depends on the protection intent. ReadOnly is used during incident investigations, cost review freezes, and compliance audits where even configuration changes could affect the evidence. CanNotDelete is the more common production choice — it prevents accidental deletion while allowing normal configuration operations like scaling, patching, and tag updates.
GET/LIST/HEAD (read operations) and all data plane
All read and write operations; only deletes are blocked
Incident freeze, billing period, compliance audit

Start with CanNotDelete for production resources. Use ReadOnly only when you specifically need to freeze all configuration changes, such as during an incident investigation or a regulatory audit period.
Auditing Lock Presence with Azure Resource Graph

Resource Graph can query for lock presence across a management group, enabling you to identify resource groups that should have a lock but do not. This is useful for compliance reporting and for verifying that a lock enforcement policy is working correctly.

# Find all resource groups that have at least one ReadOnly lock
az graph query -q "
ResourceContainers
| where type =~ 'microsoft.resources/resourcegroups'
| join kind=leftouter (
    Resources
    | where type =~ 'microsoft.authorization/locks'
    | where properties.level =~ 'ReadOnly'
    | project lockRg = resourceGroup, lockName = name, lockLevel = tostring(properties.level)
) on \$left.name == \$right.lockRg
| extend hasReadOnlyLock = isnotempty(lockName)
| project name, subscriptionId, hasReadOnlyLock, lockName
| order by hasReadOnlyLock asc
" --management-groups mg-corp -o table

# Find production resource groups (Environment=prod tag) WITHOUT a ReadOnly lock
az graph query -q "
ResourceContainers
| where type =~ 'microsoft.resources/resourcegroups'
| where tags['Environment'] =~ 'prod'
| join kind=leftouter (
    Resources
    | where type =~ 'microsoft.authorization/locks'
    | where properties.level =~ 'ReadOnly'
    | project lockRg = resourceGroup
) on \$left.name == \$right.lockRg
| where isnull(lockRg)
| project rgName = name, subscriptionId, environment = tags['Environment']
| order by subscriptionId asc
" --management-groups mg-corp -o table

# Count: how many prod RGs have a lock vs how many do not
az graph query -q "
ResourceContainers
| where type =~ 'microsoft.resources/resourcegroups'
| where tags['Environment'] =~ 'prod'
| join kind=leftouter (
    Resources
    | where type =~ 'microsoft.authorization/locks'
    | project lockRg = resourceGroup, lockLevel = tostring(properties.level)
) on \$left.name == \$right.lockRg
| summarize
    WithReadOnly   = countif(lockLevel =~ 'ReadOnly'),
    WithCanNotDelete = countif(lockLevel =~ 'CanNotDelete'),
    WithNoLock     = countif(isnull(lockLevel))
" --management-groups mg-corp -o table

When a ReadOnly lock is applied to a resource group that a CI/CD pipeline needs to update, the pipeline fails with a 409 Conflict error. The standard pattern is to remove the lock at the start of the deployment pipeline and re-apply it at the end, using a service principal that has Microsoft.Authorization/locks/delete permission. This should only be done for truly operational changes (deploying a new app version), not for configuration changes that are supposed to be blocked by the lock.

# Azure DevOps pipeline: remove lock, deploy, re-apply lock

variables:
  RESOURCE_GROUP: rg-prod
  LOCK_NAME: prod-readonly
  SUBSCRIPTION_ID: $(subscriptionId)

stages:
- stage: Deploy
  jobs:
  - job: RemoveLockAndDeploy
    steps:

    - task: AzureCLI@2
      displayName: 'Remove ReadOnly lock before deployment'
      inputs:
        azureSubscription: 'my-service-connection'
        scriptType: 'bash'
        scriptLocation: 'inlineScript'
        inlineScript: |
          set -e
          LOCK_EXISTS=$(az lock list --resource-group $(RESOURCE_GROUP) \
            --query "[?name=='$(LOCK_NAME)'] | length(@)" -o tsv)
          if [[ $LOCK_EXISTS -gt 0 ]]; then
            echo 'Removing ReadOnly lock before deployment'
            az lock delete --name $(LOCK_NAME) --resource-group $(RESOURCE_GROUP)
          else
            echo 'No lock found, proceeding with deployment'
          fi

    - task: AzureWebApp@1
      displayName: 'Deploy application'
      inputs:
        azureSubscription: 'my-service-connection'
        appName: 'app-prod-api'
        package: '$(System.DefaultWorkingDirectory)/drop/app.zip'

    - task: AzureCLI@2
      displayName: 'Re-apply ReadOnly lock after deployment'
      condition: always()  # Run even if deployment failed
      inputs:
        azureSubscription: 'my-service-connection'
        scriptType: 'bash'
        scriptLocation: 'inlineScript'
        inlineScript: |
          set -e
          az lock create \
            --name $(LOCK_NAME) \
            --resource-group $(RESOURCE_GROUP) \
            --lock-type ReadOnly \
            --notes 'Auto-re-applied by deployment pipeline'
          echo 'ReadOnly lock re-applied'

Locks do not cancel each other — they accumulate. If a ReadOnly lock is applied at the subscription level AND a CanNotDelete lock is applied at a resource group level within that subscription, the resource group has BOTH: it cannot be deleted (from the CanNotDelete lock on the RG) and its resources cannot be written to (from the ReadOnly lock inherited from the subscription). The most restrictive lock wins for any given operation.

# Scenario: subscription has ReadOnly, resource group has CanNotDelete
# Result: resources in the RG are fully locked (ReadOnly wins over CanNotDelete for writes)

# Apply ReadOnly at subscription scope (affects all resource groups)
az lock create \
  --name 'sub-incident-freeze' \
  --lock-type ReadOnly \
  --scope /subscriptions/$(az account show --query id -o tsv) \
  --notes 'Incident investigation freeze — all changes blocked'

# Apply CanNotDelete at a specific RG (protects from deletion independently)
az lock create \
  --name 'rg-cannotdelete' \
  --resource-group rg-prod \
  --lock-type CanNotDelete

# Show all locks effective for rg-prod (subscription-level + RG-level)
echo '=== Resource Group level locks ==='
az lock list --resource-group rg-prod -o table

echo '=== Subscription level locks (inherited by all RGs) ==='
az lock list --scope /subscriptions/$(az account show --query id -o tsv) -o table

# Remove ONLY the subscription-level freeze after incident resolution
# The RG-level CanNotDelete lock remains
az lock delete \
  --name 'sub-incident-freeze' \
  --scope /subscriptions/$(az account show --query id -o tsv)

# Verify: now only CanNotDelete remains for rg-prod
az lock list --resource-group rg-prod -o table

The Azure PowerShell module provides lock management commands that are useful for bulk operations — applying a lock to every resource group tagged Environment=prod across multiple subscriptions, or generating a lock inventory report for a compliance audit.

A ReadOnly lock prevents all ARM write and delete operations on the locked resource while still allowing reads and data-plane access. Apply locks at resource group scope to inherit protection across all child resources. Removing a lock requires Microsoft.Authorization/locks/delete permission (Owner or User Access Administrator — not Contributor). Key surprises: VM restart, tag changes, storage tier changes, and key rotation are all blocked because they are ARM write operations even though they feel operational. Automate lock enforcement with a DeployIfNotExists policy to ensure locks are always present on production resource groups.
